skill.name: microbiome-diversity-reporter
skill.description: Interpret Alpha and Beta diversity metrics from 16S rRNA sequencing results.
skill.summary: Automated diversity metric analysis and reporting for microbiome 16S rRNA sequencing data.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 66 patterns across 5 files. All 66 were confirmed false positives upon evaluation: Markdown backticks were misidentified as Ruby shell execution (39 instances), PCoA scientific terminology 'PC2' was misidentified as C2 keywords (3 instances), CSS styling and security checklist text were misidentified as system reconnaissance and path traversal, and report-generation file writes were misidentified as suspicious. The skill is a legitimate microbiome diversity analysis CLI tool with well-documented scope, error handling, and security boundaries. No prompt injection attempts detected. The only actual behaviors are expected filesystem writes for report generation and documented shell commands in markdown.

low_findings.title: File write to user-controlled output path
low_findings.description: The --output CLI argument is passed to open() for writing HTML, JSON, and Markdown reports. This is expected report-generation behavior for a CLI tool. The content written is scientific data analysis results, not arbitrary user input.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded source repository URL in documentation
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 7 contains a hardcoded GitHub URL pointing to the source repository. This is benign documentation attribution and not used for data exfiltration or network calls.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-controlled file write path via CLI argument
dangerous_patterns.description: The --output argument value is passed directly to open(path, 'w') without path validation. While this is standard report-generation behavior, the skill relies on the AI runtime sandbox for path safety rather than validating paths internally.

content.user_title: Analyze Microbiome Diversity from 16S rRNA Data
content.value_statement: Researchers often struggle to interpret alpha and beta diversity metrics from microbiome sequencing data. This skill automates diversity analysis and generates publication-ready reports from OTU tables.
actual_capabilities: Calculate alpha diversity metrics including Shannon, Simpson, Chao1, and Observed OTUs
Compute beta diversity distance matrices using Bray-Curtis and Jaccard methods
Perform Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) and PERMANOVA statistical testing
Generate formatted HTML, JSON, or Markdown reports with summary statistics
Produce rarefaction curves for comparing samples at different sequencing depths
limitations: Requires OTU tables in TSV format as input, not raw FASTQ sequence files
Some beta diversity metrics like weighted UniFrac need scikit-bio with phylogenetic tree support
Interactive visualizations require optional plotly installation
Cannot perform upstream processing such as quality filtering or taxonomic classification

use_cases.title: Academic Researcher
use_cases.description: A microbiome researcher analyzes 16S rRNA data from a clinical trial to compare gut diversity between treatment and control groups.

use_cases.title: Bioinformatics Student
use_cases.description: A graduate student learns diversity metric interpretation using example OTU tables and auto-generated statistical summaries.

use_cases.title: Clinical Data Analyst
use_cases.description: A clinical analyst generates standardized diversity reports for multiple patient cohorts to track microbiome changes over time.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Alpha Diversity Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate the Shannon diversity index for my OTU table at otu_table.tsv and generate an HTML report saved to alpha_report.html.

prompt_templates.title: Full Diversity Report with Statistics
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a complete alpha and beta diversity analysis on otu_table.tsv with metadata from metadata.tsv. Include PCoA ordination and PERMANOVA tests grouped by treatment. Output results as a JSON report.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive alpha and beta diversity analysis

prompt_templates.title: Custom Metric Selection
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my OTU table using Shannon, Simpson, and Chao1 metrics. Apply rarefaction to a depth of 5000 reads and generate box plots grouped by the Treatment column in my metadata. Save the report in Markdown format.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Processing Multiple Datasets
prompt_templates.prompt: Process all OTU tables in the data/ directory. For each table, compute alpha diversity with all available metrics and beta diversity with Bray-Curtis distance. Save each report with a filename matching the input sample group name.
prompt_templates.scenario: Batch processing workflow for multiple samples

output_examples.input: Calculate Shannon diversity for my gut microbiome samples and explain what the values mean.
output: Interpretation: Higher Shannon values in the control group suggest greater within-sample diversity compared to the treatment group. Values between 2.5 and 3.5 are typical for human gut microbiome samples.

output_examples.input: Run beta diversity PCoA on my samples grouped by treatment status.
output: Clear separation between Control and Treatment groups observed along PC1 axis.
PERMANOVA pseudo-F = 3.42, suggesting statistically meaningful group-level differences in microbial composition.

output_examples.input: Generate a full diversity report comparing three sample groups over a time series.
output: Shannon index increased from 2.89 (Baseline) to 3.34 (Week 12), suggesting rising microbial diversity over the study period.
PCoA shows samples clustering by time point rather than by individual, indicating a consistent temporal shift in community composition across subjects.
Statistical Note: PERMANOVA confirmed significant differences between Baseline and Week 12 (pseudo-F = 4.12).

best_practices: Validate that input OTU tables have samples as rows and OTUs as columns before running the analysis
Always provide metadata when running beta diversity to enable meaningful group comparisons
Review the statistical assumptions and limitations section of the report before citing results in publications
anti_patterns: Do not use raw FASTQ sequence files as input without first generating an OTU or ASV table
Do not interpret PERMANOVA results as definitive without verifying adequate sample size per group
Do not skip rarefaction when comparing samples that differ substantially in sequencing depth

faq.question: What input format does this skill require?
faq.answer: The skill accepts OTU tables in TSV format where rows represent samples and columns represent OTUs. An optional metadata file in TSV format enables group-level comparisons.

faq.question: Which diversity metrics are supported?
faq.answer: Alpha diversity supports Shannon, Simpson, Chao1, Observed OTUs, and Pielou evenness. Beta diversity supports Bray-Curtis and Jaccard distances, with UniFrac available when scikit-bio is installed.

faq.question: Can this skill replace full pipelines like QIIME2 or mothur?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides focused diversity analysis and reporting. Full pipelines like QIIME2 handle raw sequence processing, taxonomy assignment, and phylogenetic tree construction that this skill does not cover.

faq.question: What Python dependencies are required?
faq.answer: Python 3.8 or newer is required. Core dependencies are numpy, pandas, and scipy. Optional libraries scikit-bio, matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly enable additional visualization and metric features.

faq.question: How should I interpret a Shannon diversity index value?
faq.answer: Higher Shannon values indicate greater diversity, combining both species richness and evenness. Values typically range from 1.5 to 3.5 in human gut microbiome samples, though this varies by environment and sequencing depth.

faq.question: What does PCoA analysis show?
faq.answer: Principal Coordinate Analysis reduces complex distance matrices into visualizable two-dimensional or three-dimensional plots. Samples that appear close together have similar microbial composition, while distant samples are compositionally different.
